questions.question: Explain the fundamental difference between the Iterator and ListIterator interfaces in the Java Collections Framework. Provide a specific use case where ListIterator would be the preferred choice.
keyPoints: Iterator allows traversal in one direction (forward) only.
ListIterator is a bidirectional iterator, allowing traversal both forward and backward.
ListIterator allows modification of the list (e.g., add, set) during iteration.
Iterator is a universal cursor applicable to all Collection objects.
Use Case: ListIterator is preferred when needing to traverse a list and replace elements based on a condition, as it provides the set(E e) method.

questions.question: Describe the concept of a 'fail-fast' iterator. How does it differ from a 'fail-safe' iterator? Name one collection class that implements each behavior and explain the mechanism behind the 'fail-fast' property.
keyPoints: Fail-fast iterators immediately throw a ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified after the iterator is created.
It works by using a modCount (modification count) variable. The iterator records the expected modCount at creation and checks it on each operation.
Examples of fail-fast collections: ArrayList, HashMap.
Fail-safe iterators operate on a clone of the collection and do not throw this exception.
Example of a fail-safe collection: CopyOnWriteArrayList.
Structural modification means add, remove; updating an existing element is not structural.

questions.question: Why should the hashCode() method be overridden whenever the equals() method is overridden in a class intended for use as a key in a HashMap?
keyPoints: HashMap uses hashCode() to determine the bucket where a key-value pair should be stored.
The contract between equals() and hashCode() requires that if two objects are equal according to equals(), then their hashCode() must be the same.
Violating this contract (equal objects have different hash codes) will break the HashMap's behavior.
A key might not be found during retrieval even though an equal key exists in the map.
This ensures correct and efficient storage and retrieval of key-value pairs.
